---
title: "Session 04 teaching notes: Python 1"
session_id: session-04
event_id: null
---

### Session 4 — Python and pandas basics

TODO:

- add spyder setup
- spytertest.py (in canvas) mit studis am Anfang der python session step-by-step durchgehen
- use Anaconda (be prepared to answer questions)
- use setwd() for spyder. prepare to explain os.chdir(). Include an explicit task/test.
- check pandas version? 3.0?
- TBD: quarto-include code fragments? add explanations (asides?)

Notes:

- p.13: abhängig von Zeit: in-session or homework
- Aufgaben: komplett (soweit die Zeit da ist)
- Bei Zeitnot: Aufgaben nach Haus geben
- Python Lösungen: werden von Tutoren besprochen
- See also `Versicherungsvertreter.py` and `Versicherungsvertreter.xslx`



**Introductory discussion**

- Prior programming experience?
- Why go beyond Excel and use Python? (advanced analytics: Regression, prediction, ML, AI, ...)
- Interpreted: vs. compiled language

Python:

- motivation: existing libraries!
- Don't repeat yourself principle of programming: by using Python libraries (modularization of code), we benefit from the code written by others.
  Illustration (whiteboard): the Python code that is involved in a typical ML studyis roughly 2 million LOC. 99.99% is imported (Pandas, scikitlearn, ...) and a few hundred LOC are our code (tip of the iceberg, we orchestrate existing libraries). For the sake of the argument, we simply ignore all of the other code, the Python core or the code that runs the OS and operates the hardware.
  It is not just LOC, but the 99% of the code (the libraries) are often very well designed and maintained (if you pick the right libraries). Also, they are constantly updated so we get performance improvements, remaining bugs are fixed, and the documentation gets better.
  This means we can really "stand on the shoulders of giants". Pretty amazing.

- Why shouldn't we just use ChatGPT? (prompt adequately, validate and improve, Python: effective prompting possible)

Prepare the Python environment before the session and plan to complete as many exercises as time permits.

## Exercises

| Slide(s) | Exercise               | Summary                                                                                                          | Files                                                                                      | In-class / take-home |
| ------: | ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | -------------------- |
|       5 | Basic operators        | Run arithmetic, division, modulo, exponentiation, and string-concatenation expressions.                          | —                                                                                          |                      |
|      11 | Trace program output   | Predict the results of assignments, reassignment, multiple assignment, and arithmetic expressions.               | —                                                                                          |                      |
|      13 | Basic formula programs | Calculate a circle’s area and the future value of an investment using variables defined in the program.          | `CircleArea1.py`<br>`FutureValue.py`                                                       |                      |
|      22 | Import and export data | Load country and insurance data, inspect DataFrame dimensions and columns, and export the insurance data to CSV. | `countries.csv`<br>`countries.py`<br>`insurance.xlsx`<br>`insurance.py`<br>`insurance.csv` |                      |

## General teaching notes

- Ask about prior programming experience.
- Discuss why Python complements Excel for advanced analytics, regression, prediction, machine learning, and AI.
- Contrast interpreted and compiled languages.
- Motivate Python through reusable libraries and the “don't repeat yourself” principle.
- On the whiteboard, illustrate an ML study as a small amount of course code built on a much larger body of imported, maintained library code. Emphasize that students orchestrate existing libraries and can “stand on the shoulders of giants.”
- Ask why students should not simply use ChatGPT: effective prompting still requires validation and improvement.

::: {.callout-note title="Preparation TODOs"}
- TODO: Add the Spyder setup.
- TODO: Use Anaconda and be prepared for questions.
- TODO: Walk through `spytertest.py` from Canvas with students at the start of the Python session. Retain the original filename spelling; its repository path is not known.
- TODO: Clarify the note “use `setwd()` for Spyder.”
- TODO: Check whether pandas 3.0 is the version to discuss.
:::

## Slide 5 — Basic arithmetic and string operations

Run these expressions individually in the interactive console so that each result is visible.

```python
3*5
16-5
16/4
16/5
16%5
16//5
3**2
"Hello " + "World"
```

## Slide 6 — Operators in Python

In the preceding examples, `/` performs division, `//` performs floor division, `%` gives the remainder (modulo), and `**` performs exponentiation. For strings, `+` concatenates the operands.

## Slide 8 — Variables and reassignment

Python allows a variable to be reassigned to values of different types (dynamic typing), while multiple assignment sets several variables in one statement.

```python
x = 4
x

x = 3.12
x

x = 'hello'
x

a, b = 1, 2
a
b
a + b
```

## Slide 10 — Output with `print()`

These examples contrast comma-separated output and string concatenation, demonstrate repetition, use `str()` for conversion, and use `end` to control the line ending.

```python
print('hello')
print('hello', 'there')
print('hello' + ' ' + 'there')
print('haha' * 4)
print(5 * 4)

a = 54
print(a)
print('The number is ' + str(a) + '!')
print('The number is', a, '!')

print('hello ')
print('Peter')
print('hello', end=' ')
print('Peter')
```

## Slide 11 — Predicting program output

Predict the result of each block before running it.

```python
x = 15
x = x + 5
print(x)
```

```python
x = 4
y = x + 1
x = 2
print(x, y)
```

```python
a = 4.5
b = 2
print(a // b)
```

```python
x = 17 / 2 % 2 * 3**3
print(x)
```

```python
x, y = 2, 6
x, y = y, x + 2
print(x, y)
```

```python
a, b = 2, 3
c, b = a, c + 1
print(a, b, c)
```

The final block intentionally raises a `NameError` because `c` is read before it has been assigned.

## Slide 13 — Basic formula programs

::: {.teaching-file}
[In-class exercise]{.teaching-label .teaching-label-exercise} [`../materials/session_04/CircleArea1.py`](../materials/session_04/CircleArea1.py){.teaching-button .teaching-button-exercise}
:::

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
{{< include ../materials/session_04/CircleArea1.py >}}
```

::: {.teaching-file}
[In-class exercise]{.teaching-label .teaching-label-exercise} [`../materials/session_04/FutureValue.py`](../materials/session_04/FutureValue.py){.teaching-button .teaching-button-exercise}
:::

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
{{< include ../materials/session_04/FutureValue.py >}}
```

- Depending on available time, complete this exercise in class or assign it as homework.

::: {.teaching-break}
☕ Break — 10 minutes
:::

## Slide 15 — Importing pandas

The short alias `pd` is the conventional name used for pandas.

```python
import pandas as pd
```

## Slide 16 — Reading an Excel file into a DataFrame

The final expression displays the DataFrame in an interactive environment.

```python
data = pd.read_excel('Bikesales.xlsx')
data
```

## Slide 17 — Inspecting a DataFrame

These expressions show the DataFrame dimensions, column names, and column data types.

```python
data.shape
list(data)
data.dtypes
```

## Slide 18 — Working directory and file paths

This path is machine-specific. The raw-string prefix `r` prevents Windows backslashes from being interpreted as escape sequences.

```python
import os
os.chdir(r'D:\Dropbox\Lehre\Introduction to Programming\Presentation')
```

## Slide 19 — Reading CSV files

The relative path is resolved against the active working directory.

```python
data = pd.read_csv('Bikesales.csv')
```

## Slide 20 — Loading, previewing, and exporting data

`head()` previews the first rows; `index=False` avoids writing the DataFrame index as an extra CSV column.

```python
os.chdir(r'D:\Dropbox\Lehre\Introduction to Programming\Presentation')
exped = pd.read_excel('Exped.xlsx')
exped.head()

exped.to_csv('Exped.csv', index=False)
```

## Slide 21 — Setting a DataFrame index

After `set_index()`, `Transid` supplies the row labels.

```python
exped1 = exped
exped1 = exped1.set_index('Transid')
exped1.head()
```

## Slide 22 — Importing and exporting data

::: {.teaching-file}
[In-class exercise]{.teaching-label .teaching-label-exercise} [`../materials/data/countries.csv`](../materials/data/countries.csv){.teaching-button .teaching-button-exercise}
:::

::: {.teaching-file}
[In-class exercise]{.teaching-label .teaching-label-exercise} [`../materials/session_04/countries.py`](../materials/session_04/countries.py){.teaching-button .teaching-button-exercise}
:::

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
{{< include ../materials/session_04/countries.py >}}
```

::: {.teaching-file}
[In-class exercise]{.teaching-label .teaching-label-exercise} [`../materials/data/insurance.xlsx`](../materials/data/insurance.xlsx){.teaching-button .teaching-button-exercise}
:::

::: {.teaching-file}
[In-class exercise]{.teaching-label .teaching-label-exercise} [`../materials/session_04/insurance.py`](../materials/session_04/insurance.py){.teaching-button .teaching-button-exercise}
:::

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
{{< include ../materials/session_04/insurance.py >}}
```

- Complete all exercises as time permits.
- If time is short, assign unfinished exercises as homework.
- Python solutions will be discussed by the tutors.

::: {.callout-note title="Additional insurance material"}
The existing notes also identify these materials, but do not establish an exact page mapping:

- [`Versicherungsvertreter.py`](../materials/session_04/Versicherungsvertreter.py)
- [`Versicherungsvertreter.docx`](../materials/session_04/Versicherungsvertreter.docx)

TODO: Confirm where these materials should be used. The original note referred to the second file as `Versicherungsvertreter.xslx`; the supplied inventory contains the Word document above instead.
:::

## Summary and announcements

- TODO

::: {.callout-tip title="Notes for improvement"}
Take notes on improvements and common questions during the session and add them to `feedback.qmd` afterwards.
:::
